Despite its economic success, Fosterville remains politically and socially contentious, with community concerns over mining-induced seismic activity, environmental impacts on nearby forests and endangered species, risks to water quality and noise pollution, and stress on local residents. These issues have been examined through a formal Environmental Effects Statement inquiry, yet approvals for mine expansions such as tailings storage lifts have proceeded, intensifying debate between the company, government, and affected communities.

Site: Morwell Opencut (Lignite mine), Morwell, City of Latrobe, Victoria
metadata: Yallourn Open Cut is one of the three vast brown coal mines of Victoria’s Latrobe Valley, alongside Hazelwood and Loy Yang, forming a mining complex that covers more than 50 square kilometres—an area comparable to Sydney Harbour. The sheer scale of these open-cut mines is rarely appreciated beyond fleeting roadside or rail-line views, meaning their long-term planning, environmental management, and rehabilitation often remain out of sight, out of mind for the wider public. Geologically, the Latrobe Valley hosts around a quarter of the world’s known brown coal reserves, estimated at about 65 billion tonnes. The coal occurs in five major seams within the Traralgon, Morwell, and Yallourn formations, with individual seams reaching thicknesses of up to 100 metres. These coal measures are overlain by younger riverine sediments of the Boisdale and Haunted Hills formations, deposited around three million years ago during periods of falling sea levels, creating a complex veneer of gravels, sands, and silts that locally erode the coal. Brown coal mining began in the region in the late nineteenth century, with Yallourn operating continuously since 1921 and currently supplying fuel to electricity generation. Despite more than a century of mining, none of the major open-cut areas has been fully rehabilitated and returned to non-mining use. At Yallourn, rehabilitation works have improved stability but remain closed to public access, while at Hazelwood and Loy Yang internal dumping of overburden was delayed for decades, raising concerns about long-term landform stability. Ongoing controversies focus on the absence of proven end uses, the likelihood that mine voids will remain as partially filled socalled sunken landscapes and fears that without ambitious rehabilitation aimed at environmental, community, and economic value, these enormous sites may become enduring environmental liabilities rather than assets.

Site: Brumagen open cut, Stawell, Northern Grampians Shire, Victoria
metadata: The Brumagen Open Cut, located near Stawell in Victoria’s Northern Grampians, is a prominent legacy mining site closely associated with the historic Stawell Gold Mine. It forms part of a landscape shaped by successive waves of pastoral settlement, gold rushes, and industrial mining that transformed the district from grazing land into one of Victoria’s most important inland goldfields. Brumagen represents the later, large-scale open-cut phase of mining that followed earlier underground and shaft-based extraction during the Victorian Gold Rush, and today stands as both an industrial relic and a marker of the region’s economic development. Geologically, the Brumagen Open Cut lies within the Lachlan Fold Belt, a vast and ancient geological province extending across southeastern Australia. Formed during the Palaeozoic Era by intense folding, faulting, and magmatic activity associated with convergent plate margins, the Lachlan Fold Belt hosts complex rock structures and rich mineralisation. It is internationally recognised for economically significant deposits of gold, copper, silver, lead, zinc, and tin, and has produced some of Australia’s most important goldfields. Around Stawell, gold was discovered in quartz reefs in the 1850s, particularly along the so-called “Stawell Golden Mile,” leading to deep underground mining that later evolved into more mechanised, open-cut operations as technology advanced and shallower deposits were exhausted. Today, the Brumagen Open Cut is inactive and forms part of the Stawell Gold Mine, operated by Stawell Gold Mines Pty Ltd and indirectly owned by Arete Capital Partners. Current mining activity is focused underground, and the operator has indicated no intention to redevelop the Brumagen or Big Hill open-cut areas, instead discussing with Earth Resources Regulation the potential removal of these areas from the mining licence for future community use. Ongoing issues and controversies relate to ownership disputes among investors, long-term land use and rehabilitation outcomes, and community expectations about how this historically significant but dormant site should be managed and repurposed.

Site: Pit 7, Brockman 2 Mine, Mount Brockman, Ashburton Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Brockman No. 2 Mine is a large open-cut iron ore operation in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 60 kilometres north-west of Tom Price in the Hamersley Range, one of the world’s premier iron ore provinces. Operating since 1992, the mine is fully owned and run by Rio Tinto Iron Ore through its subsidiary Hamersley Iron Pty Ltd and forms part of Rio Tinto’s extensive Pilbara network of twelve mines. Brockman No. 2 has a production capacity of around 8.7 million tonnes of lump and fines iron ore per year. Ore is mined using conventional open-cut, truck-and-shovel methods, crushed and screened on site, and then transported by rail to the port of Dampier for blending and export. The workforce operates on a fly-in fly-out basis, supported by the nearby Brockman Airport, and the mine has experienced periods of closure, including mothballing during the global financial crisis in 2008. Geologically, the mine exploits the Brockman Iron Formation, part of the Hamersley Group, which hosts some of Australia’s most significant iron ore deposits. Mineralisation occurs as bedded iron ore within the Dales Gorge Member and as high-grade detrital ore derived from the weathering of banded iron formations. These rocks, composed mainly of hematite, magnetite, and chert, are folded into large synclinal structures, including the Brockman Syncline, and are interbedded with shale units that influence mining geometry and ore quality. Beyond its technical and economic importance, Brockman No. 2 sits within a broader landscape of social and political controversy in the Pilbara. The mine is located in a region marked by ongoing disputes over Aboriginal heritage protection, land rights, and environmental impacts. These issues, particularly affecting the Yindjibarndi people, highlight systemic weaknesses in mining approvals, cumulative impacts on cultural landscapes, water resources, and the enduring tensions between large-scale resource extraction and the rights of Traditional Owners.

Site: Lens D, Nammuldi Mine (Nammuldi Group), Mount Brockman, Ashburton Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Nammuldi Mine is a large open-cut iron ore operation in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 60 kilometres north-west of Tom Price in the Hamersley Range. It is wholly owned and operated by Rio Tinto Iron Ore through its subsidiary Hamersley Iron Pty Ltd and forms part of the company’s extensive Pilbara mining network. Production began in 2006 and the mine has an output of approximately 6.6 million tonnes of iron ore per year. Nammuldi is notable for its role in technological change in mining: from 2015, all haul trucks at Nammuldi and the nearby Yandicoogina mine have been operated remotely from Perth, around 1,200 kilometres away, making them the first fully remote-controlled iron ore trucking operations in the world. Geologically, the mine sits within the Hamersley Iron Province, an approximately 80,000-square-kilometre region that contains about 80 per cent of Australia’s identified iron ore reserves. The rocks belong to the Hamersley Group, formed around 2.5 billion years ago through chemical sedimentation in ancient oceans, with later volcanic and sedimentary inputs. Mining at Nammuldi targets the Marra Mamba Iron Formation, composed of banded iron formations rich in hematite and goethite, with alternating silica-rich layers. Nearby deposits also exploit the overlying Brockman Iron Formation, together forming some of the highest-grade iron ore resources globally. Despite its economic and technological significance, Nammuldi has been affected by controversy over Aboriginal cultural heritage protection. In August 2023, blasting near the mine damaged part of an ancient rock shelter estimated to be 40,000–50,000 years old, causing distress among the Muntulgura Guruma people. Disputes arose over the extent of the damage and whether the company adequately acknowledged the impact, reviving broader concerns about mining approvals and cultural heritage safeguards in the Pilbara.

Site: Iron King Mine, Norseman, Dundas Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Iron King Mine was a pyrite mining operation located about six kilometres south of Norseman in the Goldfields–Esperance region of Western Australia, developed to support Australia’s agricultural industry rather than gold production. Its establishment was driven by Commonwealth Government policy to encourage domestic sulphur production for sulphuric acid used in superphosphate fertiliser, implemented through the Sulphuric Bounty Act of 1954 and the Pyrite Bounty Act of 1960. Operated by Central Norseman Gold, which also ran nearby gold mines, Iron King began production in 1945, with ore transported to the Phoenix mill near Norseman after it was converted from gold to pyrite processing. Geologically, the Iron King deposit consisted of massive pyrite ore. Pyrite, chemically expressed as iron disulfide, is composed of iron and sulphur, with sulphur forming a fundamental part of the crystal structure. At Iron King, the ore averaged around 25 per cent sulphur, with estimated reserves of approximately 735,000 tonnes above a 25 per cent sulphur cut-off. Unlike the surrounding gold deposits that made Norseman one of Western Australia’s richest historic goldfields, Iron King was valued for its sulphur content rather than precious metals. The mine is remembered as much for its problems as for its production. It developed a poor safety record, with multiple fatalities recorded between the 1940s and 1950s, including a fatal fall down the main shaft in 1954. Economically, Iron King was never competitive without government support, and when cheaper sulphur became available through the processing of sour natural gas overseas, the subsidy was withdrawn. The mine closed abruptly on 31 May 1972, the same day the bounty ended. More broadly, Norseman has continued to experience mining-related safety challenges, with several fatal accidents at nearby operations in recent decades, reinforcing ongoing debates about worker safety, regulation, and the long-term vulnerabilities of small, mining-dependent communities.

Site: Mount Webber Mine, East Pilbara Shire, Western Australia
metadata: Mount Webber is an open-cut iron ore mine in the Pilbara region of Western Australia, located about 190 kilometres south-south-east of Port Hedland along the Abydos–Marble Bar Road near the boundary of the East Pilbara and Port Hedland shires. The mine was developed as a joint venture between Atlas Iron Limited and Altura Mining Limited, after Atlas acquired the iron ore rights from Haoma Mining in 2012, while Haoma retained rights to non-iron minerals. Construction cost approximately Australian dollars 210–212 million, and mining commenced in mid-2014, making Mount Webber Atlas Iron’s fifth Pilbara operation and, for a time, its primary producing asset. Ore was trucked to Port Hedland for export, with production initially around 3 million tonnes per annum and later targeted to rise to about 6 million tonnes per annum. Geologically, Mount Webber lies within the Chichester sub-region of the Pilbara, characterised by gently undulating granite and basalt plains overlain by alluvial sands, silts, and gravels. The deposit comprises the Ibanez, Fender, and Gibson ore bodies and hosts high-grade hematite mineralisation. Reported Joint Ore Reserves Committee figures indicate reserves of roughly 30 million tonnes grading about 57 per cent iron, with earlier estimates placing proven and probable reserves closer to 34 million tonnes at slightly lower grades. Mining was conducted by conventional open-pit drill, blast, load, and haul methods, with on-site crushing and screening before haulage. Mount Webber’s history has been closely tied to volatility in global iron ore markets. Less than a year after opening, operations were suspended in 2015 due to falling prices linked to oversupply and slower Chinese demand, before restarting following royalty relief from the Western Australian Government and cost reductions. Atlas Iron was later acquired by Hancock Prospecting in 2018. As Atlas’ other Pilbara mines were exhausted, Mount Webber remained one of its last operating sites, but declining production ultimately led to closure in 2024, underscoring ongoing debates about the sustainability of small, price-sensitive iron ore operations.

Site: Sally Malay Mine, Mabel Downs Station, Halls Creek Shire, Western Australia
metadata: The Sally Malay Mine, now known as the Savannah Nickel–Copper–Cobalt Mine, is a combined open-cut and underground mining operation located about 104 kilometres north-north-east of Halls Creek in the Kimberley region of Western Australia. Operated by Panoramic Resources, Savannah is unusual in that it is one of the very few nickel mines in the Kimberley, as most of Western Australia’s nickel production occurs in the Goldfields–Esperance region. Since commencing operations in the mid-2000s, the mine has experienced multiple cycles of expansion, closure, and restart, reflecting the volatility of global nickel markets. Most recently, it was placed into care and maintenance in early 2024 after Panoramic Resources entered voluntary administration, with falling nickel prices cited as the principal cause. Geologically, the Savannah deposit hosts nickel, copper, and cobalt sulphide mineralisation, initially mined by open pit and later by underground methods as operations deepened. The ore body supported significant production over time, and during its early years Savannah became the largest producer of copper in Western Australia, at times supplying up to half of the state’s output. Over its first twelve years of operation, the mine produced approximately 94,600 tonnes of nickel, 53,000 tonnes of copper, and 5,000 tonnes of cobalt. Concentrates have been exported through the port of Wyndham, with long-term offtake agreements supplying Asian markets, particularly China. Beyond economic and operational challenges, the mine has been associated with social and cultural controversy. Indigenous representatives objected to the original use of the name “Sally Malay,” arguing it breached Aboriginal cultural protocols concerning the naming of deceased people. An elder’s family publicly opposed the name as disrespectful, prompting the company’s decision to rebrand the operation as the Savannah Mine. This dispute, alongside repeated closures and workforce redundancies, highlights the complex intersection of resource development, market forces, and cultural respect in remote northern Australia.

Adi Rassi project, Dubaruba, Southern Region
metadata: The Adi Rassi deposit, 30 kilometres south of Asmara, occurs in the southern section of the Adi Rassi–Kodatu mineralised trend within the Asmara District. It contains an estimated resource of 16 million tonnes at 0.5 percent copper and 0.3 grams per tonne gold. Copper, gold and silver mineralisation is associated with foliation planes, breccia zones and quartz veins developed along a major north–south shear zone traceable for more than 3 kilometres. Mining at Adi Rassi began in the 1930s, when Italian operators excavated a series of 10 to 20 metre deep open pits along the shear zone, mainly targeting oxide copper in the South Hill zone. The deposit was again exploited briefly in the 1980s, but operations were halted by military conflict during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. Following renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000, modern exploration of the Asmara District resumed, focusing on volcanic-hosted massive sulphide copper, zinc, silver and gold systems and confirming Adi Rassi as a significant shear-hosted copper–gold target adjacent to the established volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits. These campaigns have progressively refined resources, geology and structural controls on mineralisation.

Site: Debarwa Mine, Dubaruba, Southern Region
metadata: The Debarwa deposit lies about 30 km south-southwest of central Asmara, the capital of Eritrea. It is hosted by an overturned, moderately to steeply dipping sequence of variably to intensely altered tholeiitic basaltic and rhyolitic volcanic rocks, with minor chemical sedimentary units. The exposed rusty, iron-rich, weathered caps at Debarwa attracted some of the earliest drilling in the district, both before and after Eritrean independence in 1993. The deposit was mined briefly in the 1980s, but operations ceased when military conflict intensified during Eritrea’s struggle for independence from Ethiopia. After renewed border hostilities between 1998 and 2000 subsided, systematic exploration of the Asmara district resumed. Debarwa now forms part of the Asmara Project, an advanced copper-zinc-gold-silver development that includes four main deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Most operating and exploration companies involved are Western, particularly from Canada, Australia and the UK, and their senior technical expertise largely reflects these origins. Eritreans are employed mainly in supplies, accounting, clerical roles, machinery operation and technical support. However, the bulk of the manual workforce is drawn from National Service conscripts, who have been reported to work under highly exploitative conditions, with inadequate food and poor living quarters.

Site: Emba Derho Mine, Emba Derho, Central Region
metadata: Emba Derho is a large volcanogenic massive sulphide deposit hosting gold, silver, copper and zinc, located west-north-west of Asmara. It belongs to the Asmara Project, which comprises four advanced deposits—Debarwa, Emba Derho, Adi Nefas and Gupo—and two pipeline deposits, Adi Rassi and Kodadu. Mineralisation at Emba Derho extends over about 1,250 m of strike and 850 m in width, while Adi Nefas occurs as an elongated north-northeast-trending, steeply east-dipping massive sulphide layer within a bimodal sequence of metavolcanic and metasedimentary rocks. The project is planned in three phases. Phase I began with open-pit mining at Debarwa and shipment of direct shipping ore through the port of Massawa. Phase II adds a gold heap-leach operation on near-surface ore and mining of supergene copper from Debarwa and Emba Derho, processed at a central 2 million tonnes per year flotation plant at Emba Derho, with copper concentrate plus gold and silver by-products exported via Massawa. Phase III will treat primary copper and zinc ores from Emba Derho and nearby sites, producing copper and zinc concentrates. Power is generated onsite from diesel and medium-fuel-oil generators, water is largely recycled, yet local communities question the benefits and landscape and agricultural impacts.

Site: Gupo deposit, Ad Nefas, Asmara, Central Region
metadata: The Gupo mine lies within the Asmara District of Eritrea, where volcanic-hosted massive sulphide deposits of copper, zinc, silver and gold occur close to the capital, Asmara. Gupo itself is predominantly a gold deposit hosted in highly deformed, strongly foliated mafic flows and mafic tuffs, intercalated and crosscut by thin quartz-phyric felsic flows and subordinate dykes. The highest-grade gold mineralisation occurs in crystalline, coarse-grained pyrite within quartz veins, while lower-grade gold is associated with medium- to coarse-grained euhedral to sub-euhedral pyrite in sericite-altered selvages. Gupo forms part of the Asmara Project, held by the Asmara Mining Share Company, a 60:40 joint venture between the Chinese company SRBM and state-owned ENAMCO. The project comprises four deposits—Emba Derho, Adi Nefas, Gupo and Debarwa. In the first phase, high-grade copper ore will be mined by open pit at Debarwa, containerised, and transported 120 km to the port of Massawa for shipment to a Chinese smelter. At full production, the project is expected to operate for 17 years and annually yield about 29,000 t copper, 83,000 t zinc, 42,000 oz gold and 1 million oz silver in its first eight years. Concerns have been voiced about the benefit of these operations to the local inhabitants and the impact on the surrounding landscape and agriculture.

Site: Hambok Zn-Ag Project, Gash Barka Region
metadata: The Hambok mine, a major silver- and zinc-bearing site, is one of several operations developed along Eritrea’s volcanic massive sulfide belt, where mineral extraction has a long history. After a suspension of all mineral exploration was lifted in early 2005, the Government revised its mining framework, increasing its maximum equity stake in projects to 30% from 2006 onward. Eritrea’s mining code, modelled on that of Australia’s Northern Territory, stipulates royalties of 5% on precious metals and 3% on base metals, alongside a 35% corporate tax rate. Within this framework, Sanu Resources emerged as the leading exploration landholder, with licences covering more than 2,600 km², focused largely on Hambok, its most recent volcanic massive sulfide discovery in the polymetallic belt. Drilling—7,382 m across 43 diamond drill holes—confirmed extensive massive sulfide mineralisation over more than 1,050 m of strike length and 300 m downdip, remaining open in all directions and indicating significant copper-zinc potential. Since 2012, the Hambok copper-zinc deposit has been part of the Bisha Mining Company, 60% owned by Nevsun Resources and 40% by the Eritrean National Mining Company.

Site: Harena Mine, Gash Barka Region
metadata: The Harena Mine is an open copper and zinc operation located close to the better-known Bisha site in Eritrea’s Gash-Barka region, north of the provincial capital Barentu. Developed as a satellite deposit to the northwest of the Bisha main zone, Harena is estimated, as of 2023, to contain proven and probable reserves of about 3.09 million tonnes of zinc at an average grade of 3.75% and 800,000 tonnes of copper at 0.97%. The operation is run by Zijin-Bisha Mining, an Eritrean company established to develop and operate the Bisha district mines, and jointly owned by China’s Zijin Mining Group, with a 55% stake, and the state-owned Eritrean National Mining Corporation, with 45%. Although the region is highly mineralised, it is also the agricultural heartland of Eritrea and was once heavily forested before widespread deforestation transformed the highland plateau. Politically, the country has been ruled by a single party, the People’s Front for Democracy and Justice, the successor to the Eritrean People’s Liberation Front, since independence in 1991, and continues to operate under a transitional government elected in 1993, with national elections originally scheduled for 2001 repeatedly postponed. This political context shapes governance of mining and land use practices.

Site: Ugaro Mine, Awgaro (Ugaro), Gash Barka Region
metadata: The south-western part of Eritrea is one of the most prospective gold-bearing regions in the country. The Augaro mine developed on a series of quartz veins and stringers, some showing sulphide mineralisation, formed in a low-pressure zone related to a regional shear system. The wider area hosts hundreds of small historic mines and operations that were active during the Italian occupation, which ended in 1941 with the East African campaign of World War II. Augaro was reportedly the most productive gold mine in Eritrea, and during Italian colonisation mercury amalgamation was widely used, with tailings dumped without proper waste management. Today, gold mining is increasingly informal and largely outside federal control; some projects have been overtaken by illegal exploration, extraction and smuggling, often in collusion with state and non-state actors. These activities generally ignore sustainable mining practices and worker health. A major environmental and health concern is mercury release from artisanal and small-scale mining, including in-situ evaporation from tailings near farming areas in Eritrea’s hot climate. Land degradation from unrehabilitated pits is substantial, and because small-scale mining is largely informal and miners’ awareness is limited, land reclamation remains extremely rare.
